speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, you had just described some of the conditions in the six camps that you had been interned in. And you had previously mentioned that you were liberated by the Americans on V Day, May the 8th, 1945. Can you let us know what happened to you since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- when I left the-- the hospital in Tutzing-- as I said, I went to Feldafing-- and participated in organizing and managing the camp under the authority of the military government. And there, also, I volunteered my services to the Americans. And in one instance at least, I-- I think I-- I rendered some real service, at the-- on behalf of the CIC, or what they-- the-- the counterintelligence service. Together with a friend, I managed to get ahold of the last camp commander we had, Oberscharfuhrer Meyer. And we-- we captured him in Starnberg and brought him down to the counterintelligence service. It wasn't an easy matter, because my-- my friend felt that the best treatment we could mete out to him would be to literally throw him down the mountain. But I finally succeeded in persuading him that we had better not be lawless. It's the lawlessness of the world that we suffered from. And I wasn't about to-- to become lawless or to inflict it on even a Nazi. And then at the camp, I had participated in providing the camp with food and clothing and supplies, which we could, with the authority of-- of the military government, requisition, obtain, and purchase. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also in creating a cultural life for-- for the camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had all-- all sorts of things that we did, particularly performances of all kinds. Very quickly, people of talent came forward or appeared. And we even managed at one time to take some of the scenery from the Munich opera. And-- and we had a performance of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put on in-- in Feldafing. And then, of course, there-- there was a tremendous amount of-- of work that we-- we did [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in organizing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the expression of the Zionist will and aspiration of the survivors-- organizing the very famous petition that was mentioned and preparing the-- for the first visit of Ben-Gurion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not without, by the way, conflicts. There were on the one hand communist-oriented people who were insistent on people being oriented to go back to their respective countries and build socialism there. And there were the-- the orthodox, the Hasidim, who were demanding the re-establishment of religious Judaism. And there were some quite bitter conflicts and interesting-- almost adventurous-- events in the-- in the DP camp in Feldafing, as I'm sure that there were in other camps, too. Eventually then there was the-- the organization of the various DP camps or liberated people in the various occupation of the zones, where a-- an overall organization of the liberated people was organized. And obviously, as-- as a member of the committee, I was part of that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, of course, had a tremendous advantage over 99.9% of the people because I had already known that I had at least three sisters surviving, and I had a father in-- in the United States. And I had a fixed point towards which I was gravitating. And from virtually the first day on, I was looking for ways in which I could speedily get to the United States, which was not very possible or-- or practical. But I-- I did manage it. And the way I had managed it was itself, I think, terribly meaningful to-- to the story of the post-war [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] problems of the survivors. The-- the world was not very welcoming to the survivors. Clearly, even someone like me, who had, as I say, this anchor in-- in America, could not find a way to get to America. Nobody was eager to have us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In-- in-- in fact, you know, the truth of the matter is that nobody really liberated us. Nobody fought to liberate us. We were in-- on the way, as people or as army units passed by a concentration camp, they liberated it. But nobody really made any sacrifice or any direct move to-- to liberate concentration camp prisoners. And similarly, after our liberation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a-- in a measure, of course, nobody was prepared with what they were going-- for what they were going to find and didn't know how to deal with them-- with it. And there were, of course, Jew-- Jewish organizations and UNRRA and-- and the Jewish agency that were groping towards ways of trying to-- to help. But, you know, the political considerations and complications didn't really permit-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certainly not for compensation for the suffering of these people, by the way they were being received after their survival. I'm not speaking of individual efforts that were made by various people, like the chaplains in the-- in the American army or some military governors who had a deeper understanding or a-- or a more human sympathy. But by and large, the world was closed to us. Those who wanted to go to Palestine-- of course Palestine was closed. Those who wanted to go to North America, it-- it wasn't only that there were no civilian authorities to whom you could apply for a visa. Nobody was interested and eager until we became a political nuisance over a few years that we finally began to make our-- our-- our way. Now I myself, I had left Feldafing probably sometime in August or September and wanted to make my way to Switzerland because I knew that Switzerland was the one country that was intact, and there were American representatives there. And I could begin to look for a way of getting to America. But to get to Switzerland, ultimately-- and I'm really trying to be as brief about this as I can-- I-- I had to swim over the Rhine in the middle of the night. And after a private arrangement with someone from Switzerland, I was taken to Zurich and then had to hide for it must be about three, four months in Switzerland, where there was still rationing, and where, you know, it wasn't easy to manipulate illegally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hiding and being driven from one place to another, and eventually when I needed to get a-- a transit visa to-- through France in order to get to Belgium, to Antwerp, to get on a boat, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a letter from the American consulate in-- in Zurich that they were ready to give me a visa. And because it was addressed to someone, one of the Jewish families in Zurich, the man who, when the letter was received, cut out his address, so he wouldn't be caught. And then I had folded it over, and the French consul opened it up. And he saw the hole, so he told me that he had to go and consult somebody. And when I-- and I of course immediately realized that he sensed something fishy. So while he was out of the room, I opened the window and jumped out and left. Eventually, I made my way with-- in a Belgian repatriation train, without a visa. And I jumped off the train in the first Belgian city that we arrived at, through the window and-- and then took the next train to Brussels and-- and from there to Antwerp. And it was a very complicated business of how, in one day, I had to obtain all sorts of documentation, in order to get on the boat. And then when I finally got on the boat-- and I was one of seven civilians on-- on the boat-- you have to understand that most of Europe was still in-- in shambles and-- and disarray. And there was no civilian transatlantic transport yet or any communication. What I had to go through to get the first telephone call to-- to my father was a-- is an unbelievable and-- and complicated story. But eventually, I-- I got on-- on this Liberty ship, which carried 40 American soldiers and several hundred SS dogs that were being brought as stock to-- to the United States, I presume for police purposes. And we had to ship in at about 3 o'clock in the afternoon or sometime like that, at about 7 o'clock. And this was in a-- what was a military port in Antwerp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] An American officer appeared, and the seven civilians had to come down the gangplank. And he was examining [? the ?] papers. And he takes a look at my passport, and he says, oh, enemy alien. Of course I was-- I had a Hungarian passport, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I obtained through another very adventurous story. And then I finally-- I managed to whip out the certificate that was given to me by the military governor in Feldafing. And when he looked at it, he finally told me to get back on the boat. And so I left-- it must have been in-- in the last week or so of December from Antwerp. And I arrived actually at the shores of the United States sometime in late December and was cooling my heels maybe for a week or so-- two-- I don't even know exactly how much time passed-- just outside Coney Island, in New York, where 120 ships [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were waiting for-- to be-- for berths, so that they can discharge the soldiers and-- and, you know, bring the boys home for Christmas. So that by the time I actually was admitted to the United States and, so to say, stepped on the ground of United States, it was January 6, 1946. And my-- at-- at that time my brother-in-law, who was still in the uniform of the American Merchant Marine and my sister, who was married to him, picked me up and-- and I suppose must have whisked me through the customs, et cetera. And there I was in America. And-- and that was on a Thursday, I remember. And on Sunday morning, I went to work for the Zionist Organization of America. And my saying that perhaps doesn't quite convey what I really want to say, which is that I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no problem really surviving survival, unlike some other people. I had accepted my survival, and-- because I had a purpose and a goal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my purpose and-- and goal was to reconstitute, revive, and-- and-- and assure the continuity and the permanence of Jews and Judaism. I was driven, virtually, by a-- a kind of inner compulsion about this because this I could perceive as the only purpose of survival. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If Judaism and Jews were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to survive, then what was the purpose of my survival? And the one sure way to assure the survival of the Jewish people was the-- the Zionist goal, which to me not only meant the reestablishment of-- of Jewish sovereignty, but also the reconstitution of and-- and the recreation of-- of Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I-- I-- you know, I had a-- an-- an everlasting, lifelong goal in my life, which, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, served as the purpose and-- and-- of my survival. So I had no difficulty with it. And I have, therefore, spent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nearly 50 years of my life in-- as a Jewish public servant in various organizations-- first for some 13, 14 years with the Zionist Organization of America and then for a similar number of years with B'nai B'rith. And eventually, I spent a couple of years as the executive vice president of the America-Israel Cultural Foundation and then went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Israel as the founding director of the-- the Public Affairs Department of the new university that was being built in the Negev, which eventually became Ben-Gurion University. And subsequently, eventually I came to work for the Canadian Associates of Ben-Gurion University, for about 16 or 17 years, in Montreal as-- as the national director of the Associates. Of course that, shall I say, was the-- the-- largely the intellectual side of my survival. There was of course the personal and-- and emotional side, which basically was framed in and-- and sustained by my three sisters and of course my wife, Tagora, and my two children, Ariela and Nuri, who to me-- beyond the-- in the case of my sister, it's beyond the-- the common history-- and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the very close, personal friendship, I-- I think there are no closer friends in the world that I can think of than my sisters and-- and-- and myself, and they among themselves and with me-- and my family, which to me is an essential and-- and a very central element in this perpetuation of Judaism to me. My children represent not only my children, but they represent the continuity of Judaism and of Jews. And it-- in a very real sense, these two things-- plus whatever modest contribution I have been able to make to this goal of-- of assuring the continuity of the Jewish people-- represent on the one hand, in a very real sense, a-- a demonstration of our victory over Hitler. In a way, we won. We lost a tremendous amount. And the world has lost a-- a great deal, irreplaceably. No one can bring the 6 million alive and their potential descendants and their potential achievements and contributions. But-- but we are here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a very real sense, [SPEAKING HEBREW], we are alive. [BACKGROUND NOISE] My family is an integral part of that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so I am content [? but ?] not-- not only that I had survived, but that I had lived productively and have contributed-- as I say, however modestly-- to the continuity of our people. This to me is above all the purpose of the-- of survival and, in a way, the compensation, to the extent that one can speak of compensation at all for what the Holocaust has wrought, and what we had gone through collectively and individually.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've mentioned that three of your sisters survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did not come back from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest and youngest sister of course and my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother and my youngest sister that I have told you were unfortunately murdered and-- on arrival to Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in what probably was in a way the most execrable way in which the Holocaust was operated in those days. In Auschwitz, you know, the-- the gas chambers and the-- and the crematoria were not up to handling 2/3 of Hungarian Jewry. In less than six weeks, over 600,000 people were deported. It's-- the-- the mind has to be boggled by the sheer numbers of it. But-- and so, you know, the-- the operation was if at all possible, more [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] horrible. Open fires, this was, you know-- people being shot in the air, children. [SIGHS] It has-- [SIGHS] the-- the-- the vision of that hell has-- it will never leave me, of course. As the whole Holocaust cannot leave me. I'm-- I am, in a way, as everybody else must be, imprisoned by it. And you can never be liberated from it. But in a very direct, personal way, it has prevented me for years from having a child. I could not free myself of the sight and the sound and-- and the odor and what I have seen and felt. It took-- it took really a superhuman effort on my part to finally arrive at the point where I could say to myself that I can chance bringing life, because there may be sufficient safety for it. And then, I, you know, for years, hanging on every breath [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] of my children [SOBBING] not being able to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] But thank God, thanks to them, I overcame. [SIGHS] And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a break at this moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I glory in them. No, it's all right. It's OK. [SOBS]
